description: Library of reusable analysis techniques. Each technique produces raw computed metrics — no confidence scores, no verdicts. The reasoner interprets the metrics.

anomaly_detection.name: Anomaly Detection (Z-Score + Effect Size)
anomaly_detection.description: Measures how far a numeric metric deviates from a known healthy baseline. Produces a severity score.
applicable_when: Data contains a numeric metric column with a known healthy baseline (e.g., availability %, latency ms)
You need to quantify HOW degraded a metric is, not just IF it's degraded
Dataset has at least 1 row with a numeric measurement
not_applicable_when: Data is categorical or boolean only (e.g., incident status, CritSit flag)
No meaningful baseline exists for the metric

inputs.values: Array of observed metric values (e.g., AvgValueAcrossWindows)
inputs.baseline_std: Expected healthy std dev (e.g., 0.5 for availability %)

outputs.z_score: Standard deviations from baseline (higher = more anomalous)
outputs.cohens_d: Effect size — magnitude of deviation regardless of sample size

anomaly_detection.small_sample_fallback: If row_count < 5, use simple threshold comparison (value < baseline - 2*std) instead of z-score. Cohen's d remains valid for any sample size.
anomaly_detection.example_snippet:
z_score = (baseline_mean - observed_mean) / baseline_std
cohens_d = abs(baseline_mean - observed_mean) / baseline_std
breach_ratio = len(df[df[col] < threshold]) / len(df)

temporal_overlap.name: Temporal Overlap Analysis
temporal_overlap.description: Measures time window overlap between two event sources. Determines if events are temporally correlated (co-occurring) or independent.
applicable_when: Two datasets each have start/end timestamps (or at least a start timestamp)
You need to determine if events in source A occurred DURING or NEAR events in source B
Examples: SLI breach window vs. incident window, SR filing time vs. outage window
not_applicable_when: One or both datasets lack timestamp columns
Events are instantaneous (no duration) — use proximity_scoring instead

outputs.lag_minutes: Gap between windows if no overlap (positive = A before B)

temporal_overlap.example_snippet:
overlap = max(0, min(end_a, end_b) - max(start_a, start_b))
overlap_ratio = overlap / min(dur_a, dur_b) if min(dur_a, dur_b) > 0 else 0
lag = max(start_a, start_b) - min(end_a, end_b) if overlap == 0 else 0

cross_population_comparison.name: Cross-Population Comparison
cross_population_comparison.description: Compares a single entity's metrics against an aggregate population. Determines if the entity is an outlier or part of a broader pattern.
applicable_when: You have per-entity data (one customer) AND population-level data (all customers)
You need to distinguish 'isolated anomaly' from 'widespread platform issue'
Both datasets share a common dimension (region, SLI category, product)
not_applicable_when: Only one dataset available (no population baseline to compare against)
Datasets don't share a joinable dimension

inputs.join_dimension: Column(s) to join on (e.g., region, sli_category)

outputs.population_affected_count: How many entities in the population are affected

cross_population_comparison.example_snippet:
entity_regions = set(df_entity['Region'].unique())
population_regions = set(df_population['Region'].unique())
jaccard = len(entity_regions & population_regions) / len(entity_regions | population_regions)

volume_spike_detection.name: Volume Spike Detection
volume_spike_detection.description: Detects whether the count of events (tickets, incidents, breaches) is abnormally high. Uses count-based thresholds rather than metric-level analysis.
applicable_when: Data represents discrete events (support requests, incidents, SLI breaches) rather than continuous metrics
You need to determine if 'many customers reporting' or 'many incidents open' constitutes a spike
The signal is in the COUNT of rows, not the VALUES within rows
not_applicable_when: Data is a single observation (e.g., one incident profile)
The question is about metric severity, not event volume

inputs.distinct_entities: Number of distinct affected entities (customers, regions, subscriptions)

outputs.distinct_entity_count: How many unique entities are affected
outputs.critsit_ratio: Fraction of events flagged as CritSit/high severity
outputs.severity_weighted_count: Events weighted by severity (Sev1=4, Sev2=3, Sev3=2, etc.)

volume_spike_detection.example_snippet:
distinct_customers = df['CustomerName'].nunique()
critsit_count = df['IsCritSit'].sum() if 'IsCritSit' in df.columns else 0
critsit_ratio = critsit_count / len(df) if len(df) > 0 else 0

cascade_detection.name: Cascade / Causal Lag Detection
cascade_detection.description: Tests whether degradation in source A LEADS degradation in source B in time — a proxy for causal dependency. If A degrades first and B follows, A may be causing B.
applicable_when: Two datasets each have impact start timestamps
You're testing a dependency or upstream/downstream relationship
The question involves 'is X causing Y' or 'is degradation cascading'
not_applicable_when: Events are simultaneous (no lag to detect)
The relationship is known to be independent

inputs.upstream_start: Earliest impact timestamp in the upstream/dependency source
inputs.downstream_start: Earliest impact timestamp in the downstream/primary source
inputs.shared_dimension: Region or category to verify the cascade path

outputs.temporal_order_correct: Boolean: did upstream degrade BEFORE downstream?
outputs.shared_dimension_overlap: Jaccard similarity on the cascade path dimension

cascade_detection.example_snippet:
lag = (pd.Timestamp(downstream_start) - pd.Timestamp(upstream_start)).total_seconds() / 60
temporal_order_correct = lag > 0
region_overlap = len(upstream_regions & downstream_regions) / len(upstream_regions | downstream_regions)

severity_profiling.name: Severity Profiling
severity_profiling.description: Characterizes a single entity or event by its severity attributes. Converts categorical severity fields into a numeric score.
applicable_when: Data has severity-related columns (Severity, IsOutage, IsCritSit, IsEscalated, ChildCount)
You need to profile 'how bad' a single incident, ticket, or outage is
The evidence is a single event to characterize, not a population to aggregate
not_applicable_when: Data is numeric metrics (use anomaly_detection instead)

inputs.severity: Numeric severity level (1-4, lower = more severe)

outputs.blast_radius_score: Score based on child count and related entities

severity_profiling.example_snippet:
base = {1: 1.0, 2: 0.75, 3: 0.5, 4: 0.25}.get(severity, 0.25)
boost = 1.3 if is_outage else (1.15 if is_escalated else 1.0)
severity_score = min(1.0, base * boost)

absence_quantification.name: Absence / Negative Evidence Quantification
absence_quantification.description: Scores the significance of evidence NOT being found. Important for refutation — if a thorough search found nothing, that's evidence against the hypothesis.
applicable_when: An evidence source returned 0 rows despite being expected
You need to distinguish 'no data because not collected' from 'no data because nothing exists'
The hypothesis would be weakened if this evidence category is genuinely empty
not_applicable_when: Data was not collected (ER was skipped) — use 'inconclusive' instead
Empty result is expected for this hypothesis type

inputs.was_collected: Boolean: did the data_fetcher attempt this ER?
inputs.row_count: Number of rows returned (expected: 0 for absence)
inputs.hypothesis_requires: Is this ER in the hypothesis's evidence_needed list?

outputs.was_expected: Boolean: was this ER required by the hypothesis?

breadth_scoring.name: Impact Breadth Scoring
breadth_scoring.description: Scores how widespread an impact is across multiple dimensions: regions, subscriptions, resources, SLI categories. Broader impact = higher severity.
applicable_when: Data has geographic or organizational dimensions (Region, SubscriptionId, ResourceId)
You need to quantify 'how widespread' vs. 'how severe' the impact is
Answering questions about blast radius, cross-region patterns, or multi-tenant impact
not_applicable_when: Data is a single scalar (one incident, one ticket)

inputs.dimension_columns: List of columns representing breadth (Region, SubscriptionId, etc.)

descriptive_statistics.name: Descriptive Statistics (Distribution Summary)
descriptive_statistics.description: Computes basic distribution metrics for numeric columns: mean, std, percentiles (p25, p50, p75, p90). Provides a quantitative fingerprint of the data before applying specialized techniques.
applicable_when: Data has one or more numeric columns (availability %, latency, duration, count, etc.)
You need a baseline understanding of the data distribution before deeper analysis
Useful as a first pass on any numeric dataset — always safe to apply
not_applicable_when: Data is entirely categorical or boolean (no numeric columns)

inputs.values: Array or DataFrame column of numeric values
inputs.column_name: Name of the column being summarized (for labeling)

descriptive_statistics.notes: This is a supporting technique — it characterizes the data distribution. Include its outputs in key_metrics for the ER so the reasoner has distribution context.
descriptive_statistics.example_snippet:
stats = {
    'mean': float(df[col].mean()),
    'std': float(df[col].std()),
    'p25': float(df[col].quantile(0.25)),
    'p50': float(df[col].quantile(0.50)),
    'p75': float(df[col].quantile(0.75)),
    'p90': float(df[col].quantile(0.90)),
    'min': float(df[col].min()),
    'max': float(df[col].max()),
    'count': int(df[col].count())
}

composition_rules.description: Guidelines for the sandbox coder to select and compose techniques based on available evidence.
selection_logic: For each loaded ER, inspect column types and row counts to determine applicable techniques
Numeric metric columns (Avg*, Min*, duration) → anomaly_detection or breadth_scoring
Timestamp columns in 2+ sources → temporal_overlap or cascade_detection
Event-count data (tickets, incidents) → volume_spike_detection or severity_profiling
Always apply breadth_scoring when Region/Subscription columns exist

aggregation.notes: Each technique produces raw computed metrics (z-scores, ratios, counts, overlaps). Report all metrics per ER in key_metrics. Do NOT compute confidence scores or weighted averages — the reasoner interprets the raw metrics.
